coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-based palladium complex: A versatile nano-catalyst for the green synthesis of pyrazoles
coredata.dc:creator: Ghorbani, Shiva
Habibi, Davood
Heydari, Somayyeh
Ebrahimiasl, Hakimeh
coredata.dc:description: The palladium complex based on the magnetic carbon quantum dots [Fe3O4@CQDS@Si(CH2)3 NH@CC@ATT@Pd (CQDPd = F)] was synthesized by preparation of Fe3O4, its further reactions with carbon quantum dots (CQDs), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane [Si(OEt)3(CH2)3NH2], cyanuric chloride (CC), and 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole-5-thiol (ATT), and complexation of the product with PdCl2. Then, it was characterized by several techniques such as FT-IR, XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, VSM, and used as a novel catalyst for the green synthesis of diverse pyrazoles from the reaction of aldehydes, ethyl acetoacetate and phenylhydrazine at 70 °C under solvent-free conditions with high yields and short reaction times. Aldehydes bearing the electron-withdrawing groups exhibit the superior reactivity. Remarkably, the F catalyst showed strong recyclability over six consecutive cycles with negligible loss of activity, emphasizing its sustainability and high efficiency in chemical transformations as well as the catalytic potential of the used magnetic carbon quantum dots and their ability to revolutionize green and efficient synthetic methodologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots-based palladium complex
Pyrazole
Multi component condensation reactions

Characterization by EDX and elemental mappings
Proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyrazoles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan for the support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The comparative FT-IR spectra of A, B, C, D, E and F.
The EDX and elemental mapping of F.
The VSM analysis of A, E and F.
Proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyrazoles.
Optimization of the reaction condition in the synthesis of a1.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: [a]Conditions: aldehydes (1.0 mmol), ethyl Phenylhydrazine (2.0 mmol), ethyl acetoacetate (2.0 mmol) and the pd catalyst (15 mg), at 70 °C.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different methods for the synthesis of pyrazoles a(1–12).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-based palladium complex: A versatile nano-catalyst for the green synthesis of pyrazoles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan 6517838683, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan 6517838683, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Saveh University of Medical Sciences, Saveh Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSaveh University of Medical Sciences, Saveh Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The palladium complex based on the magnetic carbon quantum dots [Fe3O4@CQDS@Si(CH2)3 NH@CC@ATT@Pd (CQDPd = F)] was synthesized by preparation of Fe3O4, its further reactions with carbon quantum dots (CQDs), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane [Si(OEt)3(CH2)3NH2], cyanuric chloride (CC), and 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole-5-thiol (ATT), and complexation of the product with PdCl2. Then, it was characterized by several techniques such as FT-IR, XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, VSM, and used as a novel catalyst for the green synthesis of diverse pyrazoles from the reaction of aldehydes, ethyl acetoacetate and phenylhydrazine at 70 °C under solvent-free conditions with high yields and short reaction times. Aldehydes bearing the electron-withdrawing groups exhibit the superior reactivity. Remarkably, the F catalyst showed strong recyclability over six consecutive cycles with negligible loss of activity, emphasizing its sustainability and high efficiency in chemical transformations as well as the catalytic potential of the used magnetic carbon quantum dots and their ability to revolutionize green and efficient synthetic methodologies.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Catalysis is a cornerstone of modern industry, with wide-ranging implications in the fields of energy conversion, environmental sustainability, and the synthesis of valuable chemicals. Over the years, researchers have attempted to enhance catalytic processes by developing materials that not only accelerate reactions, but also allow precise control over reaction pathways. Magnetic catalysts have demonstrated the unique capability, a feature that opens new avenues for catalytic selectivity and efficiency. At the same time, quantum dots, with their size-dependent electronic and optical properties, offer the advantage of tailoring catalysts to specific applications, optimizing both the catalytic activity and selectivity [1–3].

In the realm of modern chemical synthesis, the pursuit of efficient and environmentally friendly catalytic methodologies is a continuous endeavor. The emergence of nanomaterial-based catalysts has revolutionized the landscape of chemical transformations, offering enhanced reactivity and selectivity, reduced waste generation, and economically sustainable alternatives to conventional catalysts. One such promising nanocatalyst on the forefront of research is magnetic carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have recently attracted significant attention for their outstanding catalytic properties in countless chemical reactions [4,5]. They have remarkable physicochemical features like low toxicity, inexpensive, strong, and broad optical absorption, high chemical stability, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, rapid electron transfer properties and high photostability. They have bright fluorescence, are resistant to bleaching and are capable of emitting fluorescent light of different wavelengths. These properties make them an excellent tool for visualization of brain structures and underlying mechanism of its function. Also, due to their unique properties, even single studied molecules can be observed [6–15].

Also, the catalytic application of CQDs is an interesting area of study, with diverse applications across various domains of synthetic chemistry. Through an in-depth investigation, we will delve into the synthesis and characterization of CQDs, their unique catalytic features, and the mechanistic insights that underpin their catalytic efficiency. Moreover, we will discuss the recent advancements and applications in this emerging field, highlighting the potential of CQDs to revolutionize the landscape of catalytic chemistry. Also, CQDs have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in facilitating the synthesis of organic compounds, a class of molecules renowned for their wide-ranging biological and pharmacological significance. Magnetic CQDs, as a subset of CQDs, combine the intrinsic properties of carbon dots with the additional advantage of magnetism, making them a versatile and unique class of nanocatalysts. CQDs are carbon-based nanomaterials with exceptional photophysical properties, chemical stability, and biocompatibility. When coupled with magnetic functionality, CQDs not only offer excellent catalytic performance but also the convenience of easy separation and recycling from reaction mixtures using external magnetic fields [16–19].

Schiff bases represent a well-documented class of compounds characterized by the presence of the CN functional group. They hold a prominent position in the realm of organic chemistry and have emerged as versatile and widely employed organic molecules. The increasing recognition of Schiff bases can be attributed to their extensive array of biological activities and their multifaceted applications across diverse scientific disciplines, encompassing medicinal, pharmaceutical, analytical, and inorganic chemistry [20–23]. Beyond their significance in these fields, Schiff bases serve as valuable catalysts and play central role as intermediates in organic synthesis. Furthermore, their utility extends to the production of various industrial commodities, including dyes, pigments, polymer stabilizers, and corrosion inhibitors. This broad range of applications underscores the pivotal role that Schiff bases occupy in contemporary chemistry, making them a subject of enduring interest and research [24–26].

Heterocyclic compounds with pyrazole moieties, exhibit a wide range of biological and pharmaceutical properties such as antipyretic, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory effects [27], antibacterial and antifungal activities [28], antioxidant properties [29], hypoglycemic potential [30], antiproliferative attributes [31], antiviral efficacy [32], and even anticancer potential [33]. These versatile compounds have also found utility as inhibitors of mycobacterium tuberculosis [34], constituents of dyestuffs [35], and key ingredients in pesticide formulations [36].

Among the various methods for their synthesis, one of the most practical and efficient protocols involves a one-pot five-component condensation reaction of phenylhydrazine (2 mmol), ethyl acetoacetate (2 mmol) and arylaldehydes (1 mmol) [37,38].
This paper aims to study preparation of F as below (Scheme 1 ):

Then, F was used as a capable nano-catalyst for the green synthesis of diverse pyrazoles with potential therapeutic applications such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-cancer activities at 70 °C and solvent-free conditions (Scheme 2 ).

All reagents were purchased from international chemical suppliers and used without further purification. Melting points were determined using a BUCHI 510 apparatus with open capillary tubes. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in KBr pellets employing a Perkin Elmer GX FT‐IR spectrometer. The NMR spectra were recorded using a 250 MHz BRUKER AVANCE instrument in DMSO-d6 . Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images were generated using a KYKY EM3200 instrument, operating at a 30 kV accelerating voltage. High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was conducted with a HRTEM Philips CM30 instrument at 300 kV. Energy-Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was carried out using a FESEM-SIGM (German) instrument. Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) was performed with a TGA PYRIS DIAMOND instrument. Magnetic properties of the catalyst were measured using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) on an MDKFT instrument.
Synthesis of F was performed via the following six sequential stages:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stage one: synthesis of Fe3O4 MNPs (A)
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of A performed by the method previously reported [39]. Briefly, FeCl3·6H2O (0.02 mol) and FeCl2·4H2O (0.01 mol) were dissolved in water (80 mL). The pH of the solution was adjusted to about 10 by adding of 37 % ammonia solution (10 mL), and the mixture was heated at 80 °C under a N2 atmosphere for 0.5 h.
A was isolated as a black solid using a magnet, washed with water, and air-dried.

Edible oil (20 mL) was gradually heated until it reached to 250 °C. Glucose (10 g) was added and maintained at this temperature until the color of the mixture turned black (about 15 min). The mixture was then allowed to cool to room temperature, water (20 mL) and diethyl ether (20 mL) were added, and the obtained CQDs in the aqueous phase was isolated by decanting. Water removed and the obtained CQDs were kept for further reactions [39].
Next, A (1.0 g) was dispersed in water (50 mL) by ultrasonication for 15 min, CQDs (0.05 g) was added and stirred for 24 h at room temperature.
B was separated by a strong magnet, thoroughly washed with water, and oven dried at 60 °C.
B (1.0 g) was dispersed in toluene by ultrasonication for 15 min, and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (2 mL) was gradually added to the mixture and refluxed for 24 h.
C was separated by a strong magnet, washed with toluene, and oven dried at 60 °C.
Cyanuric chloride (CC, 6 g, 32 mmol) was stirred in dry THF (50 mL) for 3 h at 0 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. C (1.0 g) was added and the mixture further stirred at room temperature for 24 h.
D was separated by a strong magnet, washed with THF and ethanol and oven dried at 60 °C.

D (1.0 g) was dispersed in toluene for 15 min, and the mixture of 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole-5-thiol (2.78 g, 24 mmol) and potassium carbonate (3.3 g, 24 mmol) in toluene (70 mL) were added to the dispersed D and refluxed for 24 h.
E was separated by a strong magnet, washed with DMSO and oven dried at 60 °C.
E (1.0 g) were added to PdCl2 (2.11 g, 12 mmol), dispersed in toluene and the reaction mixture was heated for 24 h at 70 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere while being stirred.
F was separated by a strong magnet, washed with ethanol, and oven dried at 60 °C.

Phenylhydrazine (2.0 mmol), ethyl acetoacetate (2.0 mmol), benzaldehyde (1.0 mmol), and F (15 mg) were mixed, heated at 70 °C under solvent-free conditions, and progress of the reaction monitored by TLC. Then, the mixture allowed to cool to room temperature, F was separated by a magnetic and the analyses were performed using FT-IR, 1H NMR, and 13C NMR techniques.

Fig. 1 displays six different FT-IR spectra which correspond to A-F. In A spectrum, the peak at about 577 cm−1 is attributed to the FeO stretching vibration. Spectrum B showss peaks at 1707 and 3411 cm−1 correspond to the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of CQDs. Spectrum C shows peaks at 1081, 2964 and 3425 cm−1 which are attributed to the SiO, CH, and NH2 bonds, respectively. The peak at 1161 cm−1 in D spectrum corresponds to the CN bond in cyanuric chloride. Three peaks in E spectrum at 1151, 1618 and 2780 cm−1 correspond to the CN, NN, and SH bonds. In the F spectrum, the shift of the peaks indicates the formation of the palladium complex [42].

Fig. 2 displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks of F at specific 2θ angles, specifically at 30, 35, 40, 43, 53, 57, 62, 68, 71 and 74°. These peaks align with the anticipated pattern associated with the structure of iron oxide nanoparticles [39]. The presence of well-defined and sharp peaks indicate the crystalline nature of F and the atoms are organized and highly ordered, emphasizing the structured and crystalline composition of the catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization by EDX and elemental mappings
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the EDX spectrum of F which provides information about the elemental composition of the sample by detecting characteristic X-rays emitted when the sample is exposed to X-ray radiation. The spectrum reveals the presence of specific elements in F, namely oxygen, iron, silicon, nitrogen, sulfur, and palladium. The identification is based on the characteristic X-ray peaks associated with each element. The mapping images in Fig. 3 also indicate that the O, Fe, Si, N, S, and Pd elements are homogeneously distributed in F.

The images in Fig. 4 were obtained using SEM which is a high-resolution microscopic technique that provides detailed images of the surface morphology of materials at different level. As shown, the F particles have spherical shapes and the size range of these spherical particles are relatively small and falls within the range of 42–66 nanometers.

The TEM images provide valuable information about the internal features of a sample, including crystal structure, morphology, and other fine details. Fig. 5 shows TEM images of F with a nanometer-sized core-shell structure, which represents a configuration where one material (core) is surrounded by another material (shell).
Fig. 6 shows a nice decrease (74, 55 and 45 emu/g) indicating the gradual coating of A to F by different layers. These results demonstrate the magnetic properties of all three compounds.
While the magnetic property of F is reduced compared to A, the magnetic value is still sufficient for F to be effectively separated from the reaction mixture by a powerful magnet.

Fig. 7 shows the TGA curve of F and the weight losses occur during increasing the temperature. The initial decrease in weight loss below 150 °C is attributed to the water molecules and organic solvents. The second weight loss between 290 and 380 °C, is most likely associated with the thermal decomposition of various layers surrounding the Fe3O4 core. The next weight loss after 500 °C is probably attributed to decomposition of the main Fe3O4 core [41].

The reaction condition was optimized for the green synthesis of a1 in the five-component condensation reaction of benzaldehyde (1.0 mmol), ethyl acetoacetate (2.0 mmol), and phenyl-hydrazine (2.0 mmol) as a model reaction in different conditions of solvents, temperatures, and amount of F (Table 1 ). The best condition was found to be a 1:2:2 molar ratio of benzaldehyde, ethyl acetoacetate, and phenylhydrazine in solvent-free conditions with 15 mg of F at 70 °C.
Solvent-free conditions are often preferred due to reduced environmental impact. The use of solvents, especially organic solvents, can cause pollution and environmental hazards.

Practically, we observed that the solvent-free condition is the best condition. Now we can conclude that since our catalyst acts as an acid, so the solvents (especially polar solvents) will block our catalyst to reduce its reactivity and in fact the situation would be better without the solvent.

Based on the results from the model reaction, the wide range of pyrazoles were synthesized from the reaction of ethyl acetoacetate and phenylhydrazine with different aldehydes carrying electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups. In general, aldehydes carrying the electron-withdrawing groups, especially in para positions, produced higher yields in short reaction times compared to aldehydes carrying the electron-donating groups (Table 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyrazoles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyrazoles by F.
First, the nucleophilic attack of the amino group of phenylhydrazine to the carbonyl group of the activated ethyl acetoacetate will yield the intermediate one.
Second, the internal cyclization of the intermediate one with deletion of ethanol will produce the intermediate two. The subsequent keto-enol tautomerism, will form the intermediate three.
Third, the intermediate four is the result of the nucleophilic attack of the intermediate three to the activated aldehyde, which by deletion of water, the intermediate five will be formed.
Finally, the nucleophilic attack of the intermediate three to two molecules of the intermediate five and the relevant keto enol tautomerization will form the product [43].

One of the important factors in the design of environmentally friendly nanocatalyst systems is the ability to recycle and reuse the catalyst. In order to investigate the recyclability and reusability of F, the reaction of phenylhydrazine, ethyl acetoacetate, benzaldehyde, and F for the synthesis of a1 was chosen as the model reaction under optimal conditions. After completion of the reaction, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the nanocatalyst was separated from the reaction mixture by an external field, washed with water/ethanol, air dried and reused in the next reaction runs. Recyclability of F was assessed via the six stages, and the results showed that there was no significant loss of activity (Fig. 9 ).
Table 3 shows the comparison of F with other catalysts reported in literature for the synthesis of pyrazoles to indicate the capability of F in terms of time and efficiency.

The present methodologies exhibit several advantages, including higher yields, shorter reaction times, milder reaction conditions, improved scalability, and novelty of the F catalyst. However, in future research, there are opportunities to explore the potential limitations associated with current approaches and suggestions aimed at advancing the synthesis of pyrazole derivatives.

In conclusion, this study shows the synthesis, characterization and application of F, as a magnetic CQDs. The successful synthesis of CQDs F was confirmed by various characterization techniques, demonstrating their unique structural properties. Also, the applicability F was investigated in the green synthesis of diverse pyrazoles to prove the capability of F. One of the most significant findings of this study is the robust recyclability of the catalyst F, to maintain its catalytic activity in six consecutive cycles with negligible loss, indicating its potential, sustainability and efficiency in chemical transformations.
Shiva Ghorbani: Investigation, Formal analysis. Davood Habibi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Somayyeh Heydari: Methodology. Hakimeh Ebrahimiasl: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Hereby, as a corresponding author, I (Davood Habibi) am responsible for ensuring that all the descriptions in this manuscript are accurate and agreed by all authors in such a manner to meet the standard of the Journal.
Also, the authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan for the support of this work.
Shiva Ghorbani: PhD student and the lab researcher (shiva.ghorbani31@gmail.com & sh.ghorbani@che.basu.ac.ir).
Davood Habibi: Supervisor and the project administrator (emails: davood.habibi@gmail.com & dhabibi@basu.ac.ir).
Somayyeh Heydari: Scientific advisor (emails: somayyehhe4@gmail.com & somi.heydari@ basu.ac.ir).
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.137892.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First report of the application of simple molecular complexes as organo-catalysts for Knoevenagel condensation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Panja, N. Dwivedi, S. Saha, First report of the application of simple molecular complexes as organo-catalysts for Knoevenagel condensation. RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 65526–65531.
A.F. Trindade, P.M. Gois, C.A. Afonso, Recyclable stereoselective catalysts. Chem. Rev. 109 (2009) 418–514.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recyclable catalysts for the synthesis of heterocyclic compounds using carbon materials

S. Sadegh-Malvajerd, Z. Arzehgar, F. Nikpour, Regio-and chemoselective synthesis of 5-aroyl-NH-1,3-oxazolidine-2-thiones. Z. Naturforsch. B 68 (2013) 182–186.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper-based Schiff base complex immobilized on core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2 as a magnetically recyclable and highly efficient nanocatalyst for green synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ebrahimiasl, D. Azarifar, Copper-based Schiff base complex immobilized on core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2 as a magnetically recyclable and highly efficient nanocatalyst for green synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes. Appl. Organomet. Chem. 34 (2020) e5359.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ebrahimiasl, D. Azarifar, J. Rakhtshah, H. Keypour, M. Mahmoudabadi, Application of novel and reusable Fe3O4@Co(II) (macrocyclic Schiff base ligand) for multicomponent reactions of highly substituted thiopyridine and 4H-chromenes, Appl. Organomet. Chem. 34 (2020) e5769.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: s-Triazinium-based ionic liquid immobilized on silica-coated Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles: an efficient and magnetically separable heterogeneous catalyst for synthesis of 2-amino-4,8-dihydropyrano [3,2-b]pyran-3-carbonitrile derivatives for antioxidant and antifungal evaluation studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Azarifar, H. Ebrahimiasl, R. Karamian, M. Ahmadi-Khoei, s-Triazinium-based ionic liquid immobilized on silica-coated Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles: an efficient and magnetically separable heterogeneous catalyst for synthesis of 2-amino-4,8-dihydropyrano [3,2-b]pyran-3-carbonitrile derivatives for antioxidant and antifungal evaluation studies. J. Iran. Chem. Soc. 16 (2019) 341–354.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of Fe3O4-supported Schiff base Cu(II) complex: a novel efficient and recyclable magnetic nanocatalyst for one-pot three-component synthesis of quinolin-5-one, chromene -3-carbo-nitrile and phthalazine-5,10-dione derivatives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ebrahimiasl, D. Azarifar, M. Mohammadi, H. Keypour, M. Mahmoodabadi, Synthesis of Fe3O4-supported Schiff base Cu(II) complex: a novel efficient and recyclable magnetic nanocatalyst for one-pot three-component synthesis of quinolin-5-one, chromene -3-carbo-nitrile and phthalazine-5,10-dione derivatives. Res. Chem. Intermed. 47 (2021) 683–707.
